title: What grit for MDF before painting?
problem: MDF can show fuzzy edges, scratches, or uneven paint if it is not sanded correctly.
likely_cause: MDF faces and edges behave differently. Edges absorb more finish and can get fuzzy if sanded too aggressively.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for MDF faces. Use 220 or 320 lightly on sealed MDF edges.
steps: Seal cut edges before final sanding.
Lightly sand sealed edges with 220 or 320.
avoid: Do not wet sand raw MDF.
success_check: The MDF is smooth, dry, and dust-free before primer or paint.
quick_answer: For MDF before painting, sand faces with 180 or 220. Seal edges, then sand them lightly with 220 or 320.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not wet sand raw MDF.
Do not leave MDF dust on the surface before primer.
search_phrases: what grit for MDF before painting

title: What grit for plywood edges?
problem: Plywood edges can feel rough and show glue lines after cutting.
likely_cause: Layered plywood edges expose different grain directions and glue lines, so they need gradual smoothing.
recommended_grit: Use 120 for rough saw marks, then 180 and 220 for prep.
steps: Keep the sanding block flat to avoid rounding the edge too much.
avoid: Do not start with ultra-fine grit on rough plywood edges.
success_check: The edge feels even and has no sharp splinters or saw marks.
quick_answer: For plywood edges, use 120 for rough saw marks, then 180 and 220 for final prep.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not press hard enough to chip veneer layers.
Do not round edges unless that is the intended shape.

title: Can I sand laminate furniture before painting?
problem: Paint may not grip laminate furniture if the glossy surface is not scuffed.
likely_cause: Laminate is a hard, slick surface. It needs light scuff sanding and proper cleaning before primer.
recommended_grit: Use 220 or 320 to scuff laminate before primer.
steps: Apply a primer made for slick surfaces.
avoid: Do not sand through the laminate layer.
success_check: The surface is clean, dull, and evenly scuffed without exposed substrate.
symptom: paint will not stick to laminate
quick_answer: Yes. Clean laminate, then scuff lightly with 220 or 320 before primer. Do not sand through the laminate.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use 80 or 120 grit on thin laminate.
Do not skip cleaning before scuff sanding.
search_phrases: can I sand laminate furniture before painting

title: What grit to sand a sticking door edge?
problem: A door rubs, sticks, or scrapes against the frame.
likely_cause: The edge needs controlled material removal and smoothing so the door can close without rough marks.
recommended_grit: Use 80 or 120 for material removal, then 180 or 220 to smooth the edge.
steps: Remove material with 80 or 120 only on the high spot.
Clean dust before repainting or sealing.
avoid: Do not sand the whole door edge when only one high spot is rubbing.
success_check: The door closes freely and the sanded edge feels smooth.
quick_answer: For a sticking door edge, use 80 or 120 on the high spot, then smooth with 180 or 220.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not remove more material than needed.
Do not leave bare wood unsealed after sanding.
search_phrases: what grit to sand sticking door

title: What grit for refinishing stair treads?
problem: Stair treads can show worn finish, scratches, and uneven bare wood during refinishing.
likely_cause: Old finish and traffic wear need removal first, then a wood prep sequence before staining or coating.
recommended_grit: Use 80 or 100 for worn finish removal, then 120, 180, and 220 for prep.
steps: Remove worn finish with 80 or 100 where needed.
Vacuum and wipe dust before finish.
avoid: Do not leave coarse grit scratches before staining or clear coating.
success_check: The tread is smooth, even, and free of old finish ridges.
quick_answer: For stair treads, remove worn finish with 80 or 100, then prep with 120, 180, and 220.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not skip from 80 directly to stain.
Do not leave dust in corners before coating.
search_phrases: what grit for refinishing stair treads

title: What grit for splintered deck boards?
problem: Deck boards feel rough, splintered, or weathered.
likely_cause: Outdoor wood fibers lift and break down from wear, moisture, and sun exposure.
recommended_grit: Use 60 or 80 for heavy splinters, then 100 or 120 for smoother prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry after the wood is fully dry.
steps: Let the deck boards dry completely.
Clean dust before stain or sealer.
avoid: Do not sand wet deck boards.
success_check: The boards feel smoother and no loose splinters remain.
quick_answer: For splintered deck boards, sand dry wood with 60 or 80, then smooth with 100 or 120 before sealing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use ultra-fine grit for rough outdoor wood.
Do not seal over loose dust.
search_phrases: what grit for splintered deck boards

title: What grit for sanding a cutting board?
problem: A cutting board can feel rough, stained, or knife-marked after use.
likely_cause: Knife marks and raised fibers need smoothing before applying food-safe oil or finish.
recommended_grit: Use 120 for knife marks, then 180 and 220 for smoothing.
steps: Clean and dry the board completely.
Sand knife marks with 120 if needed.
Remove dust before applying food-safe oil or finish.
avoid: Do not sand a wet cutting board.
success_check: The board feels smooth with no deep knife ridges before oiling.
quick_answer: For a cutting board, use 120 for knife marks, then 180 and 220 before applying food-safe oil or finish.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not leave sanding dust on the board.
Do not use very coarse grit unless there are deep knife marks.
search_phrases: what grit for sanding cutting board

title: What grit for butcher block countertops?
problem: Butcher block countertops can show scratches, water marks, or dull worn areas.
likely_cause: The surface needs even sanding across the affected area before oil or finish is applied.
recommended_grit: Use 120 for visible scratches, then 180 and 220 for final smoothing.
steps: Remove dust before applying the chosen oil or finish.
avoid: Do not sand only one small spot without blending the surrounding area.
success_check: The countertop has an even feel and appearance before oil or finish.
quick_answer: For butcher block, use 120 for visible scratches, then 180 and 220 before oil or finish.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not leave a low spot by sanding one area too much.
Do not apply oil over sanding dust.
search_phrases: what grit for butcher block countertops

title: What grit for sanding wood filler?
problem: Wood filler can stay raised, rough, or visible after finishing.
likely_cause: Filler must be leveled after it dries and then blended into the surrounding wood scratch pattern.
recommended_grit: Use 120 or 150 to level dried filler, then 180 and 220 to blend.
avoid: Do not sand soft or uncured filler.
success_check: The filled area feels level with the surrounding wood.
quick_answer: Let wood filler dry, level it with 120 or 150, then blend with 180 and 220.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand filler before it cures.
Do not polish raised filler with fine grit instead of leveling it.
search_phrases: what grit for sanding wood filler

title: Wood grain raised after staining
problem: The wood feels rough or fuzzy after stain dries.
likely_cause: Liquid stain can raise wood fibers, especially if the surface was not prepped evenly before finishing.
recommended_grit: Use 320 lightly after the stain dries, then clean dust before the next finish layer.
wet_or_dry: Dry after the stain is fully dry.
steps: Lightly scuff the raised fibers with 320.
Remove dust before applying the next finish layer.
avoid: Do not sand through the stain color.
success_check: The surface feels smoother without removing visible stain color.
quick_answer: After stain dries, lightly scuff raised grain with 320. Use light pressure so you do not sand through the color.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand wet stain.
Do not use coarse grit after stain is applied.

title: How to sand polyurethane drips
problem: Polyurethane drips or runs leave raised marks in the finish.
likely_cause: A thick finish area must cure first, then be leveled before the next coat.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 after the drip is fully cured. Use 600 for finer blending before another coat.
wet_or_dry: Dry for most polyurethane correction; wet only if the finish instructions allow it.
steps: Level the raised drip with 320 or 400.
avoid: Do not sand soft polyurethane drips.
success_check: The drip no longer feels raised and the area is ready for the next coat.
quick_answer: Let polyurethane drips cure fully, then level with 320 or 400 and clean dust before recoating.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand tacky polyurethane.
Do not sand through the surrounding finish.

title: What grit to sand paint runs?
problem: Paint runs or sags leave raised lines after the paint dries.
likely_cause: The raised paint must harden before it can be leveled without smearing.
recommended_grit: Use 600 to level small runs, then 800 or 1000 for finer blending before polishing or recoating.
wet_or_dry: Wet for fine paint correction after paint has cured.
steps: Wet sand the raised run with 600.
Polish or recoat based on the paint system.
avoid: Do not sand fresh or soft paint runs.
success_check: The run is level and the sanding haze is even.
quick_answer: Let paint runs cure, then wet sand with 600, refine with 800 or 1000, and polish or recoat.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand soft paint.
Do not press hard on the raised run.
search_phrases: what grit to sand paint runs

title: What grit for sanding body filler?
problem: Body filler can stay uneven or show scratches under primer.
likely_cause: Filler needs shaping with coarser grit, then refinement before primer.
recommended_grit: Use 80 or 120 for shaping, then 180, 220, and 320 before primer.
avoid: Do not wet sand uncured or porous body filler.
success_check: The filler is level and the scratch pattern is fine enough for primer.
quick_answer: For body filler, shape with 80 or 120, refine with 180 and 220, then finish with 320 before primer.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not skip refinement before primer.
Do not leave filler dust on the repair area.
search_phrases: what grit for sanding body filler

title: What grit for automotive primer before paint?
problem: Paint can show primer scratches or poor surface texture after spraying.
likely_cause: Primer needs to be sanded fine enough for topcoat while staying clean and even.
recommended_grit: Use 400 or 600 before paint. Use 800 for finer wet prep when the paint system calls for it.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on the primer and paint system.
steps: Use 800 for finer wet prep when needed.
avoid: Do not paint over coarse primer scratches.
success_check: The primer is smooth, clean, and evenly dull before paint.
quick_answer: For automotive primer before paint, use 400 or 600. Use 800 for finer wet prep when the paint system requires it.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not spray paint over dusty primer.
Do not use coarse 180 or 220 scratches as final primer prep.
search_phrases: what grit for automotive primer before paint
sand primer before car paint grit

title: What grit to sand clear coat runs?
problem: Clear coat runs leave raised glossy lines that remain visible after curing.
likely_cause: The raised run needs careful leveling and fine wet sanding before polishing.
recommended_grit: Use 1000 to level carefully, then 1500, 2000, and 3000 before polishing.
steps: Let the clear coat cure fully.
Wet sand the raised run carefully with 1000.
Polish after the haze is even.
avoid: Do not sand through the clear coat around the run.
success_check: The run is level and the area has an even fine haze before polishing.
quick_answer: Let clear coat cure, then wet sand the run with 1000, refine with 1500, 2000, and 3000, then polish.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand uncured clear coat.
Do not stay on one spot long enough to cut through.
search_phrases: what grit to sand clear coat runs
clear coat run wet sanding grit

title: What grit to blend touch-up paint?
problem: Touch-up paint can leave a raised edge or visible blob after it dries.
likely_cause: The touch-up area needs to be leveled and refined before polishing or recoating.
recommended_grit: Use 1500 or 2000 for careful leveling, then 3000 before polishing.
steps: Let the touch-up paint cure fully.
Polish after the area is level.
avoid: Do not sand fresh touch-up paint.
success_check: The raised edge is level and the haze is small and even before polishing.
quick_answer: For touch-up paint, let it cure, wet sand lightly with 1500 or 2000, refine with 3000, then polish.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use coarse grit on small touch-up paint spots.
Do not sand outside the repair area more than needed.
search_phrases: what grit to blend touch up paint
wet sand touch up paint grit

title: What grit removes surface rust from tools?
problem: Hand tools have light surface rust or dark oxidation.
likely_cause: Rust needs mechanical removal, then finer grits to reduce scratch marks on the tool surface.
recommended_grit: Use 220 or 320 for surface rust, then 400 or 600 for smoother finish.
wet_or_dry: Dry for rust removal; wet can be used at finer stages after loose rust is gone.
steps: Wipe loose dirt from the tool.
Clean and protect the metal after sanding.
avoid: Do not use very coarse grit on precision surfaces unless heavy rust requires it.
success_check: Loose rust is removed and the surface has an even scratch pattern.
quick_answer: For light tool rust, use 220 or 320, then refine with 400 or 600 and protect the metal after sanding.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not leave sanding residue on tools.
Do not use aggressive grit on cutting edges unless reshaping is needed.
search_phrases: what grit removes surface rust from tools
clean rust from tools with sandpaper

title: What grit removes metal burrs?
problem: A cut metal edge has sharp burrs or rough spots.
likely_cause: Cutting or drilling metal leaves raised edges that need leveling before fine finishing.
recommended_grit: Use 120 or 180 for burr removal, then 220, 320, or 400 to smooth the edge.
wet_or_dry: Dry for burr removal; wet can be used for finer finishing.
avoid: Do not leave a sharp edge after sanding.
success_check: The edge feels smooth and safe with no raised burrs.
quick_answer: For metal burrs, use 120 or 180 to remove the burr, then smooth with 220, 320, or 400.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not drag loose metal grit into fine finishing steps.
Do not sand without checking the edge by feel carefully.

title: What grit for brass or copper polishing prep?
problem: Brass or copper still shows scratches before polishing.
likely_cause: Soft metals need scratches refined in small steps before polish can create shine.
recommended_grit: Use 600, 800, 1000, 1500, 2000, then 3000 before polishing.
steps: Start with 600 if scratches are visible.
Polish after the scratch pattern is uniform.
avoid: Do not polish over visible 600-grit scratches.
success_check: The surface has a uniform fine haze before polishing.
quick_answer: For brass or copper polishing prep, refine through 600, 800, 1000, 1500, 2000, and 3000, then polish.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not skip fine grits before polishing soft metal.
Do not use dirty slurry during final stages.

title: Can I sand chrome?
problem: Chrome can scratch, dull, or flake if it is sanded incorrectly.
likely_cause: Chrome is a thin plated surface. Sanding can cut through the plating or create permanent visible scratches.
recommended_grit: Avoid sanding decorative chrome. For damaged chrome that will be refinished, use 800, 1000, 1500, and 2000 carefully.
wet_or_dry: Wet for fine correction work on damaged chrome.
steps: Identify whether the chrome is decorative and still intact.
Do not sand intact decorative chrome for routine cleaning.
For refinishing work, wet sand carefully with 800 or finer.
Refine through 1000, 1500, and 2000 before the next repair step.
avoid: Do not use coarse grit on chrome plating.
success_check: The chrome is not cut through and the scratch pattern is controlled.
quick_answer: Do not sand intact decorative chrome for routine cleaning. For refinishing damaged chrome, use wet 800 or finer only.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use 120, 220, or 400 grit on decorative chrome.
Do not sand chrome when simple cleaning is the real need.

title: What grit to scuff powder coat before painting?
problem: Paint may not stick well to a glossy powder-coated surface.
likely_cause: Powder coat is hard and slick, so it needs cleaning and an even scuff before recoating.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 to scuff powder coat before primer or paint.
wet_or_dry: Dry for scuff prep; wet can be used for finer finishing.
steps: Apply primer or paint according to the coating instructions.
avoid: Do not paint over glossy unscuffed powder coat.
success_check: The powder coat is clean, dull, and evenly scuffed.
quick_answer: To paint over powder coat, clean first, then scuff evenly with 320 or 400 before primer or paint.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not use coarse grit that cuts through good coating unnecessarily.
Do not skip degreasing before sanding.
search_phrases: what grit to scuff powder coat
paint over powder coat sanding grit

title: What grit for PVC pipe edges?
problem: Cut PVC pipe can have burrs, sharp edges, or rough plastic flakes.
likely_cause: Sawing or cutting PVC leaves raised plastic burrs that need light smoothing.
recommended_grit: Use 120 or 180 to remove burrs, then 220 or 320 for a smoother edge.
steps: Wipe the pipe clean before assembly or finishing.
avoid: Do not remove too much material from a fitting area.
success_check: The pipe edge feels smooth and has no loose burrs.
quick_answer: For PVC pipe edges, remove burrs with 120 or 180, then smooth with 220 or 320.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not over-sand pipe ends that need to fit tightly.
Do not create heat by pressing hard.
search_phrases: what grit for PVC pipe edges

title: What grit for plastic bumper scratches?
problem: A plastic bumper has scratches that remain visible before primer or paint.
likely_cause: Plastic bumper repair needs the scratch leveled, then refined so primer can cover the sanding marks.
recommended_grit: Use 320 or 400 for scratch prep, then 600 for finer primer prep.
wet_or_dry: Dry for shaping; wet can be used at 600 for finer finishing.
avoid: Do not use heavy pressure that heats or gouges the bumper plastic.
success_check: The scratch is leveled and the surrounding area is evenly feathered.
quick_answer: For plastic bumper scratches, use 320 or 400, feather the area, then finish with 600 before primer.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not start with 80 or 120 on bumper plastic.
Do not prime over loose plastic dust.
search_phrases: what grit for plastic bumper scratches

title: What grit for resin 3D print support marks?
problem: Resin 3D prints have small bumps or marks where supports were removed.
likely_cause: Support points need gentle leveling and fine smoothing to avoid flattening small details.
recommended_grit: Use 400 or 600 for support marks, then 800, 1000, and 1500 for finer smoothing.
wet_or_dry: Wet for fine smoothing after the print is fully cured.
steps: Make sure the resin print is fully cured.
avoid: Do not sand uncured resin dust without proper protection and dust control.
success_check: Support marks are blended without losing surrounding detail.
quick_answer: For resin 3D print support marks, use 400 or 600, then refine with 800, 1000, and 1500.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not flatten small details by over-sanding.
Do not skip dust control when sanding resin.
search_phrases: what grit for resin 3D print support marks

title: What grit for spackle before paint?
problem: Spackle patches can show raised edges or rough texture under paint.
likely_cause: The patch needs to be leveled and feathered into the surrounding wall before primer or paint.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for dried spackle. Use 320 lightly for final feathering if needed.
steps: Feather the edges into the wall.
Remove dust before primer or paint.
avoid: Do not sand wet or soft spackle.
success_check: The patch edge cannot be felt by hand before painting.
quick_answer: For spackle before paint, let it dry, sand with 180 or 220, feather the edges, and remove dust.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not over-sand into drywall paper.
Do not paint over spackle dust.
search_phrases: what grit for spackle before paint

title: What grit between coats of joint compound?
problem: Joint compound ridges can show through the next coat or final paint.
likely_cause: High spots and ridges need light leveling, not aggressive sanding, between coats.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 lightly between coats. Use 220 or 320 for final smoothing.
steps: Let the joint compound dry completely.
Knock down ridges lightly with 180 or 220.
Remove dust before the next coat.
avoid: Do not over-sand into drywall paper.
success_check: Ridges are leveled and the patch is ready for the next coat.
quick_answer: Between coats of joint compound, use 180 or 220 lightly. For final smoothing, use 220 or 320.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand aggressively between coats.
Do not apply the next coat over loose dust.
search_phrases: what grit between coats of joint compound
sand drywall compound before next coat

title: Can sandpaper smooth tile edges?
problem: Cut tile edges can feel sharp or slightly rough.
likely_cause: Hard tile edges need silicon carbide abrasive and light wet sanding to smooth small rough spots.
recommended_grit: Use 220 or 320 for rough ceramic edges, then 400 or 600 for smoother edges.
steps: Wet sand the rough edge with 220 or 320.
Rinse away grit before checking by touch.
avoid: Do not use sandpaper to reshape large tile cuts.
success_check: The edge feels smoother and no sharp burrs remain.
quick_answer: Silicon carbide sandpaper can smooth small rough tile edges. Wet sand with 220 or 320, then 400 or 600.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not expect sandpaper to replace a tile saw or grinder for major shaping.
Do not dry sand tile dust without dust control.
search_phrases: what grit for ceramic tile edge

title: Can sandpaper sharpen chisels?
problem: A chisel edge is dull and needs sharpening or edge refinement.
likely_cause: A flat abrasive surface can refine a metal edge when the angle is held consistently.
recommended_grit: Use 400 or 600 for edge repair, then 1000, 1500, and 2000 for sharpening refinement.
wet_or_dry: Dry or wet depending on the sharpening setup.
steps: Attach the sandpaper to a flat surface.
Hold the chisel bevel at a consistent angle.
Remove the burr from the back side.
avoid: Do not sharpen on a soft or uneven backing.
success_check: The bevel is even and the edge feels clean and sharp.
quick_answer: Yes. Use sandpaper on a flat surface: 400 or 600 for repair, then 1000, 1500, and 2000 for refinement.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not round the bevel by changing the angle.
Do not use loose sandpaper without a flat backing.
search_phrases: sharpen chisel with wet dry sandpaper

title: How to clean sanding dust before painting
problem: Paint can look gritty, fisheye, or peel when sanding dust remains on the surface.
likely_cause: Loose dust blocks adhesion and creates small defects under primer or paint.
recommended_grit: After final sanding, keep the same final prep grit and focus on dust removal before coating.
wet_or_dry: Dry dust removal after dry sanding; clean wipe after wet sanding.
steps: Vacuum or brush off loose dust.
Wipe with a clean dry or slightly damp cloth suited to the surface.
Paint only when the surface is clean and dust-free.
avoid: Do not paint over visible sanding dust.
success_check: A clean hand or cloth picks up little to no dust before painting.
quick_answer: Before painting, remove loose dust, wipe clean, let the surface dry, and paint only when it is dust-free.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not paint over powdery sanding dust.
Do not trap wet residue under paint.
search_phrases: how to clean sanding dust before painting

title: What grit to sand walls before repainting?
problem: Repainted walls can show bumps, glossy spots, or poor adhesion when the surface is not prepped.
likely_cause: Old paint, small ridges, and patch edges need light scuffing so the new paint lays evenly.
recommended_grit: Use 180 or 220 for patched areas and 220 or 320 for light scuff sanding on painted walls.
steps: Sand patched or rough areas with 180 or 220.
Scuff glossy painted areas with 220 or 320.
Remove dust before primer or paint.
avoid: Do not paint over glossy spots, dust, or raised patch edges.
success_check: The wall feels smooth, clean, and evenly dull before repainting.
quick_answer: For walls before repainting, use 180 or 220 on patched areas and 220 or 320 for light scuff sanding.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not over-sand into drywall paper.
Do not paint over sanding dust.
search_phrases: what grit to sand walls before repainting
